<!--
PURPOSE

A reviewer reads this description, then walks the commits one-by-one.
The description's job is to load them with just enough context to
spot HIGH-LEVERAGE issues (wrong interface, wrong abstraction, wrong
approach) BEFORE deep-reading any function body. It is not a tour,
not a narrative, not a re-statement of the diff.

The mental model is a whiteboard. A smart coworker explaining the
change sketches the old shape, redraws what changed, writes the new
method names, notes a decision in the corner, and circles the
hairiest spot. Then they hand you the marker. That is the target —
the shape of a 60-second standup at a board, not a paragraph.

Goal: scannable in 30 seconds. The reviewer must be able to flag a
wrong decision without reading a single function body.

SKIP-IF-EMPTY (the load-bearing rule)

An empty or padded section is worse than no section. If a section's
content would be vague, generic, or a restatement of the title /
diff / commits, DELETE THE ENTIRE SECTION — heading and all. The
order below is fixed; sections you skip simply do not appear.

Do not pad. Do not add a section to look thorough. Do not add
"Summary", "What changed", "Overview", or "Generated by" sections.
The title is the summary; commits are the changelog; tools do not
need credit.

SIZE AS SIGNAL

More than 5 entries under Decisions, or more than ~10 lines of code
under Public surface, is evidence the PR is too big. Pause and
consider splitting before continuing.

WRITING

Concise. One concrete fact per sentence or bullet. No metaphors,
no "really" / "very" / "simply" / "just", no decorative adjectives.
Concrete nouns over hand-waving ("validates input order" not
"improves robustness").

GUIDANCE COMMENTS

The HTML comments under each heading explain when to include the
section and the shape its contents should take. After filling a
section, you may leave the guidance comment in place (it renders
invisibly on GitHub) or remove it.
-->

## Why

<!--
1-2 sentences. The motivation, constraint, or anchoring decision the
reviewer cannot infer from the diff.

Skip the section if the title already conveys it.

Bad:  "This PR refactors workspace ignore handling."
      (Restates the title.)
Good: "Gitignore parsing was duplicated at three call sites; we want
      it in one place so future filters can layer on top."
      (Names the constraint.)
-->

## Shape

<!--
Old → new at the STRUCTURAL level: where code lives, what owns what,
how runtime flow moves. Not behavior, not implementation detail.

Skip the section if there is no architectural delta (pure bugfix,
small rename, docs-only).

Form — default to a paired before/after diagram. Skip the diagram
only when the change is genuinely small (pure rename, single-file
bugfix, docs-only, mechanical type fix); in that case 3-5 bullets
are enough. When in doubt, draw the diagram.

Diagram rules:
  - PAIRED before/after — two diagrams, one labeled "Before", one
    labeled "After". Never a standalone "new shape"; the delta is
    the point.
  - COLOR-CODED to show what changed vs. what was already there:
      green fill  = added in this PR
      red fill    = removed in this PR
      yellow fill = changed in this PR
      default     = pre-existing, unchanged
    Include a one-line legend under the diagrams.
  - ANNOTATIONS on arrows and nodes — name the load-bearing
    relationships ("injected by caller", "returns", "extends",
    "<<unexported>>"). Bare class boxes do not earn their place.
  - Mermaid for general use; ASCII for tiny shapes (≤4 nodes).
  - ≤8 nodes per diagram. If you need more, either the PR or the
    diagram is wrong.
  - One verb per arrow type ("uses" / "depends on" / "calls" —
    pick one).
  - Each diagram captioned with the delta it shows.
  - No nested subgraphs beyond one level. No general-architecture
    portraits unrelated to this PR's change.

A short prose lead-in (1-3 bullets) under the diagrams is welcome
when the picture alone leaves something implicit.
-->

## Where it lives

<!--
Repo-root file-structure sketch for the important changed areas.
This is placement review, not a complete file list.

Include when changes span multiple packages/directories, add/remove/
move files, or introduce a new ownership boundary.

Skip for single-file fixes, obvious small renames, docs-only edits,
or when paths add no review signal.

Form: embedded Change Tree SVG first, then the bare Change Tree in
a collapsed details fallback. Do not leave the bare tree as the
primary content.

Author the Change Tree as a Unicode filesystem tree anchored at repo
root. Use `.` as the root. Include unchanged parent directories as
context; annotate only nodes that matter for ownership or review.

Legend: `++` added, `**` changed, `~~` moved, `--` removed.

Generate the embed with the CLI only:

  npx change-tree-svg embed -f tree.txt

Copy/paste the command's generated `<pre>...</pre>` output exactly as
the first content in this section. Do not write, edit, upload, or
assemble SVG/image/embed markup by hand.

Then add the same tree as a fallback:

  <details>
  <summary>Text fallback</summary>

  ```text
  .
  ├── apps/
  │   └── cli/
  │       └── src/
  │           └── ** register-context-command.ts # wires history flag
  └── packages/
      └── backend-typescript/
          └── src/
              ├── ++ reference-search/
              │   └── ++ ... 12 files           # owns TS ref lookup
              └── ~~ git/
                  └── ~~ ... 3 files            # moved from apps/cli/src/history/
  ```

  </details>

Raw Change Tree example:

  .
  ├── apps/
  │   └── cli/
  │       └── src/
  │           └── ** register-context-command.ts # wires history flag
  └── packages/
      └── backend-typescript/
          └── src/
              ├── ++ reference-search/
              │   └── ++ ... 12 files           # owns TS ref lookup
              └── ~~ git/
                  └── ~~ ... 3 files            # moved from apps/cli/src/history/

Rules:
  - Show important changed areas, not every touched file.
  - Use `... N files` for collapsed groups. Count files, even when
    the group is generated or mechanical.
  - Place `~~` moved entries at their new path and note the old path:
    `# moved from <old path>`.
  - Use `**` only for changed existing files whose placement or role
    matters.
  - Include tests only when their placement carries review signal.
    Collapse colocated tests into the source group when they follow
    the same ownership.
  - Inline `# ...` comments should name purpose or ownership. Do not
    restate marker status except the required moved-from note.
  - The fallback tree must match the rendered Change Tree.
-->

## Public surface

<!--
Verbatim signatures of new / changed / removed exports. Code, not
English. The reviewer reads code faster than prose and can flag a
wrong interface in seconds.

Skip the section if the PR adds or changes no public surface.

Form: TypeScript signatures, no bodies. Group by added / changed /
removed if more than one category is present.

  Added:
    class WorkspaceIgnore {
      constructor(rootDirectory: string);
      matches(path: string): boolean;
    }

  Changed:
    // before: walk(rootDirectory: string): string[]
    walk(rootDirectory: string, ignore: WorkspaceIgnore): string[]

More than ~10 lines here is a size-signal — the PR is probably too
big to review in one sitting.
-->

## Decisions

<!--
≤5 entries. Each is a load-bearing judgment call — an interface
choice, an abstraction choice, an approach choice, a placement
choice. The kind of thing where if the reviewer disagrees, the PR
needs to change shape, not just nits.

Form, one per entry:
    Chose X over Y, because Z.

The alternative Y must be one you ACTUALLY CONSIDERED. If you cannot
name a real alternative, the decision was not load-bearing — do not
list it.

Omit the entire section when no decisions meet that bar. This is
common for bugfixes, renames, and mechanical refactors.

Bad:  "Chose to use a class."
      (No alternative named.)
Good: "Chose a class with constructor injection over a module-level
      singleton, because tests need to swap in a fake without
      monkey-patching."

More than 5 real decisions is a size-signal — split the PR.
-->

## Look here

<!--
≤3 pointers to spots the reviewer should examine more carefully:
suspicious caching, race conditions, edge cases you were unsure
about, things you would want a second opinion on.

Each pointer is a file:line reference, one line each. The actual
concern goes as an inline review comment on the PR after it opens,
anchored to the exact line — not in this body.

Skip the section only if you would defend every line of this PR.
At least one pointer is usually honest; agents are bad at admitting
uncertainty, so err toward listing one when in doubt.
-->

## Reading order

<!--
Commit list with one line each, placing each commit in the arc of
the PR. Lets the reviewer hold the global thread while walking
commits.

Skip the section if the PR is a single commit.

Form:
  1. <commit title> — role of this commit in the arc.
  2. <commit title> — role of this commit in the arc.

Each caption is the commit's role in the bigger story, not a
restatement of the commit message. "Adds the class itself" is fine;
"Implements WorkspaceIgnore class with caching, matching, and path
normalization" is not — that's the commit message's job.
-->
